design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect scattered bones from Elm Street locations, defeat Freddy Krueger boss in each area to unlock new zones and earn keys for progression
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Sleep Meter mechanic forces constant resource management—falling asleep transports all players to vulnerable Dream World where Freddy is more dangerous
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Ordinary teenager(s) investigating and dismantling the threat of Freddy Krueger by gathering his bones for disposal, with cooperative multiplayer reinforcing group survival horror
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unique Sleep Meter creates asymmetric threat (single player falling asleep affects all players in multiplayer), forcing both individual vigilance and group accountability. Dream World transitions serve as escalating difficulty spikes that restructure the threat landscape rather than simple enemy stat increases

difficulty.lives_system: Player loses a life after being attacked a certain number of times; multiple lives per character
difficulty.checkpointing: Bones must be collected within each building/area before Freddy boss appears; no mid-level checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Sleep Meter management provides player agency—staying in motion slows meter drain; coffee pickups can be strategically collected. Multiplayer adds emergent difficulty through coordination challenges, as one player's sleep status affects all players

progression.upgrade_system: Dream World power-ups grant temporary special abilities (shurikens, javelins, magic projectiles) during vulnerable state transitions
progression.unlock_mechanism: Keys earned by defeating Freddy bosses unlock new areas of Elm Street initially locked due to midnight setting; progression gated by boss defeats rather than level design alone
replayability_hooks: Multiplayer cooperative gameplay up to 4 players with NES Four Score changes group dynamics and timing challenges
Dream World sequences introduce variable difficulty spikes based on Sleep Meter depletion timing
Multiple character control (ordinary teenagers) implies different character mechanics though documentation limited

world_building_techniques: Midnight setting naturally gates area accessibility, reinforcing horror atmosphere
Enemy variety (zombies, cats, dogs, skeletons, bats, rats, spiders, minotaurs) creates supernatural tone aligned with Freddy Krueger franchise
Dream World visual distinction (movielike wavering screen effect per reviewers) reinforces narrative stakes of sleep threat
Objective framing (collecting bones to rebury/dispose of Freddy in high school furnace) provides clear narrative justification for level structure

boss_design.philosophy: Freddy Krueger serves as gating mechanism for area progression; boss victories unlock new zones and provide narrative milestone (bone collection → furnace disposal chain). Reviews note Freddy felt 'bizarrely weak' despite being the mechanical threat, suggesting design prioritizes Sleep Meter tension over individual boss complexity
notable_bosses: Freddy Krueger (multiple encounters per area, becomes more dangerous in Dream World)

faction_indicators: Multiplayer cooperation against Freddy Krueger creates shared enemy faction
Teenagers vs. Supernatural threat (Freddy + hostile creatures) establishes implicit social alignment
danger_signals: Sleep Meter depletion as visible threat indicator affects all players simultaneously
Dream World transition represents escalating danger state requiring group adaptation
Scattered bones create resource scarcity requiring coordination to collect before boss confrontation
environmental_storytelling: Locked areas at midnight create urgency and exploration motivation
Coffee pickups scattered throughout levels signal resource availability for specific threat (sleep vulnerability)
High school furnace as final destination carries cultural/horror significance (Freddy's origin mythology)

sources_used: wikipedia: A Nightmare on Elm Street (video game)
